perturbation.original_text: (e) to provide proper and secure storage for each and every part of the Confidential Information received by it in tangible form whilst it is in its custody, power or control;
perturbation.changed_text: (e) to provide storage for each and every part of the Confidential Information received by it in tangible form whilst it is in its custody, power or control; and to take steps to secure such storage as it deems reasonably necessary;
perturbation.explanation: The addition of 'and to take steps to secure such storage as it deems reasonably necessary' introduces ambiguity and makes the obligation less strict. The original text requires 'proper and secure storage,' while the modified text makes the security measures subjective and based on the Recipient Party's assessment of what is 'reasonably necessary.'
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 32 of the GDPR requires the implementation of appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk. Allowing the Recipient Party to determine what is 'reasonably necessary' contradicts the GDPR's requirement for appropriate and specific security measures to protect personal data contained within the confidential information, as it allows for potentially insufficient security measures.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Art. 32 GDPR – Security of processing - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Taking into account the state of the art, the costs of implementation and the nature, scope, context and purposes of processing as well as the risk of varying likelihood and severity for the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the controller and the processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk, including inter alia as appropriate: the pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data; the ability to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of processing systems and services; the ability to restore the availability and access to personal data in a timely manner in the event of a physical or technical incident; a process for regularly testing, assessing and evaluating the effectiveness of technical and organisational measures for ensuring the security of the processing. In assessing the appropriate level of security account shall be taken in particular of the risks that are presented by processing, in particular from accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to personal data transmitted, stored or otherwise processed. Adherence to an approved code of conduct as referred to in Article 40 or an approved certification mechanism as referred to in Article 42 may be used as an element by which to demonstrate compliance with the requirements set out in paragraph 1 of this Article. The controller and processor shall take steps to ensure that any natural person acting under the authority of the controller or the processor who has access to personal data does not process them except on instructions from the controller, unless he or she is required to do so by Union or Member State law. Suitable Recitals ( 75 ) Risks to the Rights and Freedoms of Natural Person

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the security measures for confidential information, potentially contradicting GDPR Article 32. The original text mandates 'proper and secure storage,' whereas the modified version allows the Recipient Party to decide what is 'reasonably necessary,' which may not meet the 'level of security appropriate to the risk' as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1: 'the controller and the processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk.' This subjectivity weakens the security requirements and creates a contradiction because the GDPR requires appropriate and specific measures, not just what the Recipient Party deems 'reasonably necessary.'

perturbation.original_text: (f) to return or destroy or if stored in electronic form to erase from its systems (to the extent possible) at its own expe nse the Confidential Infor mation of the Disclosing Party together with any copies in its possession or in the possession of its Representatives at the request of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: (f) to return or destroy or if stored in electronic form to erase from its systems (if practical) at its own expense the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party together with any copies in its possession or in the possession of its Representatives at the request of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'to the extent possible' to 'if practical' introduces ambiguity. 'To the extent possible' implies a diligent effort to erase data, whereas 'if practical' introduces a subjective assessment of feasibility, potentially allowing the Recipient Party to avoid erasure based on inconvenience or cost.
perturbation.contradicted_law: GDPR - Article 17 - Right to Erasure ('Right to be Forgotten')
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 17 of the GDPR grants individuals the right to have their personal data erased. By changing the requirement to erase data 'to the extent possible' to 'if practical', the modified clause introduces a loophole that could allow the Recipient Party to avoid complying with the right to erasure if they deem it impractical, undermining the GDPR's mandate.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Art. 17 GDPR – Right to erasure (‘right to be forgotten’) - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay and the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay where one of the following grounds applies: the personal data are no longer necessary in relation to the purposes for which they were collected or otherwise processed; the data subject withdraws consent on which the processing is based according to point (a) of Article 6 (1), or point (a) of Article 9 (2), and where there is no other legal ground for the processing; the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (1) and there are no overriding legitimate grounds for the processing, or the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (2); the personal data have been unlawfully processed; the personal data have to be erased for compliance with a legal obligation in Union or Member State law to which the controller is subject; the personal data have been collected in relation to the offer of information society services referred to in Article 8 (1). Where the controller has made the personal data public and is obliged pursuant to paragraph 1 to erase the personal data, the controller, taking account of available technology and the cost of implementation, shall take reasonable steps, including technical measures, to inform controllers which are processing the personal data that the data subject has requested the erasure by such controllers of any links to, or copy or replication of, those personal data. Paragraphs 1 and 2 shall not apply to the extent that processing is necessary: for exercising the right of freedom of expression and information; for compliance with a legal obligation which requires processing by Union or Member State law to w

perturbation.justification: The modification from 'to the extent possible' to 'if practical' introduces a significant ambiguity that undermines the GDPR's 'right to erasure'. Snippet 1 explicitly states 'the data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay and the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay'. The 'if practical' clause allows the Recipient Party to potentially avoid this obligation based on their subjective assessment, creating a loophole that contradicts the GDPR mandate. This is not just a minor softening, it introduces an escape clause that significantly weakens the legal requirement of erasure. The change directly affects the enforceability of the erasure obligation, thereby creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Disclosure to Third P arties The Recipient Party shall have the rig ht to disclose the Confidential Information to a third party involved in the Purpose, subject to the Recipient Party obtaining the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party to such disclosure and the Recipient Party agrees that it shall procure that th e third party shall first enter into a written acknowledgement and undertaking of confidentiality in favour of both Parties to this Agreement and in a form which is acceptable to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Disclosure to Third P arties The Recipient Party may disclose the Confidential Information to a third party involved in the Purpose, provided the Recipient Party attempts to obtain the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party to such disclosure and the Recipient Party agrees that it will encourage that the third party to first enter into a written acknowledgement and undertaking of confidentiality.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'shall have the right to disclose... subject to obtaining prior written consent' to 'may disclose... provided the Recipient Party attempts to obtain' significantly weakens the requirement for consent. Similarly, changing 'procure' to 'encourage' makes the third party's confidentiality agreement non-mandatory.
perturbation.law_explanation: If the Confidential Information contains personal data and the third party processes this data on behalf of the Recipient Party, the third party becomes a 'processor' under GDPR. Article 28 requires a legally binding contract between the controller (Recipient Party) and the processor (third party) with specific provisions for data protection. By only requiring an 'attempt' to get consent and 'encouraging' a confidentiality agreement, the modified text fails to ensure a legally binding contract with adequate data protection provisions as mandated by Article 28.
perturbation.justification: The change weakens the obligation for obtaining consent and ensuring a confidentiality agreement. The original text mandates 'shall have the right to disclose... subject to obtaining prior written consent' and that the Recipient Party 'shall procure' a confidentiality agreement. The modified text changes this to 'may disclose... provided the Recipient Party attempts to obtain' consent and 'will encourage' a confidentiality agreement. This introduces ambiguity and weakens the legally binding nature of the agreement, creating a legal contradiction as it makes the obligations non-mandatory and open to interpretation.

perturbation.original_text: 4.3 The Recipient Party may disclose Confidential Information to the extent required: (a) by any order of any court o f competent jurisdiction or any competent judicial, governmental, regulatory or supervisory body; (b) by the rules of any listing authority, stock exchange or any regulatory or supervisory body with which the Recipient Party is bound to comply; or (c) by applica ble laws or regulations, provided that before it discloses any Confidential Information the Recipient Party will, to the extent permitted by applicable law and regulation, inform the Disclosing Party of the full circumstances and the information require d to be disclosed, consult with the Disclosing Party as to possible steps to avoid or limit disclosure, take such of those steps as the Disclosing Party may reasonably require and, where the disclosure is to be by way of a public announcement, make reasona ble efforts to agree the wording of the announcement with the Disclosing Party in advance.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.3 The Recipient Party may disclose Confidential Information to the extent required: (a) by any order of any court o f competent jurisdiction or any competent judicial, governmental, regulatory or supervisory body; (b) by the rules of any listing authority, stock exchange or any regulatory or supervisory body with which the Recipient Party is bound to comply; or (c) by applica ble laws or regulations, provided that before it discloses any Confidential Information the Recipient Party will, to the extent permitted by applicable law and regulation, consider informing the Disclosing Party of the circumstances, consult with the Disclosing Party as to possible steps, and where the disclosure is to be by way of a public announcement, attempt to agree the wording of the announcement with the Disclosing Party in advance.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'inform the Disclosing Party' to 'consider informing', 'take such of those steps as the Disclosing Party may reasonably require' to removing it altogether, and 'make reasonable efforts to agree' to 'attempt to agree' introduces significant ambiguity. These changes make the prior consultation and collaboration with the Disclosing Party discretionary rather than obligatory.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 152 of the Irish Data Protection Act 2018 requires controllers to implement appropriate measures to safeguard personal data. If the confidential information contains personal data, the Recipient Party's failure to make reasonable efforts to consult with the Disclosing Party before disclosing the information pursuant to legal requirements could be deemed a failure to implement appropriate measures to protect the data subject's rights and freedoms, especially the right to confidentiality. The weakening of the obligation to consult increases the risk of non-compliance with Section 152.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Chapter 8 – Remedies, liability and penalties - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Article 77 Right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority Article 78 Right to an effective judicial remedy against a supervisory authority Article 79 Right to an effective judicial remedy against a controller or processor Article 80 Representation of data subjects Article 81 Suspension of proceedings Article 82 Right to compensation and liability Article 83 General conditions for imposing administrative fines Article 84 Penalties GDPR Table of contents Report error
perturbation.justification: The original contract language mandated specific actions ('inform,' 'take,' 'make efforts'), whereas the changed language introduces ambiguity by using discretionary terms ('consider informing,' 'attempt to agree'). This weakening of the obligation to consult, especially concerning confidential information which may contain personal data, increases the risk of non-compliance. The Law Explanation, referencing Section 152 of the Irish Data Protection Act 2018, highlights the potential failure to implement appropriate measures to protect data subjects' rights, particularly confidentiality. Article 82 from Scraped Law Snippet 2 implies liability can arise. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 provides no additional information, Snippet 2 referencing Article 82 reinforces the legal ramifications of failing to adequately protect data.
